title: Panchindiya Sutra (Shri Guru-Sthapana Sutra)
summary: Panchindiya is a short two-verse Prakrit hymn that lists the thirty-six virtues (chhattis guna) of an ideal Jain guru. It is recited to mentally 'install' or invoke the guru — to establish the sthapanacharya, a symbolic seat of the guru's presence — before beginning pratikraman or samayika. In plain terms, the reciter pictures a true teacher defined by self-control and right conduct, and bows to that ideal so the whole rite is performed under the guru's guidance. A newcomer can read it as a compact portrait of what makes a worthy spiritual teacher.
ritualPlacement: In the Achhalgach (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha) order, Panchindiya is recited near the very start of the Daivasika (Devasi) pratikraman, just after the Navkar Mahamantra, as the guru-sthapana — the act that establishes the sthapanacharya (the symbolic guru-seat) so the rest of the rite proceeds before the guru. It belongs to the third Avashyaka, Vandanaa (veneration of the guru). As a shared sutra it recurs across the daily and periodic pratikramans (devasi, rai, pakshik, chaumasi, samvatsari); Chaumasi and Samvatsari carry no independent text of their own but follow the Pakshik procedure with the standard word-substitution (pakkhi → chaumasi → samvatsari) and the prescribed kayotsarga override. This placement and substitution rule is stated here once and is not repeated in the per-verse commentaries.
authorAndSource: — Traditional anonymous Prakrit gatha-pair long current in the Shvetambara pratikraman liturgy; no individual author is named in the source book Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition). The verses simply enumerate the canonical thirty-six guru-attributes and should be treated as inherited liturgical text rather than an attributed composition.
structureArc: The two verses are a single arithmetic argument that the guru possesses thirty-six virtues. Verse (1) counts the first eighteen: control of the five senses (panchindiya sanvarano), the ninefold guard of celibacy (navaviha bambhachera guttidharo), and freedom from the four passions (chauviha kasaya mukko) — 5 + 9 + 4 = 18, closed by the phrase 'iy attharasa gunehim sanjutto' (thus joined with eighteen virtues). Verse (2) adds the next eighteen: the five great vows (pancha mahavvaya jutto), competence in the fivefold conduct (pancha vihayara palana samattho), the five carefulnesses (pancha samio) and the three restraints (tigutto) — 5 + 5 + 5 + 3 = 18 — summed in 'chhattis gunehim guru majjha' (with thirty-six virtues, [such is] my guru). Read together, 18 + 18 = 36.
recurringImagery: The sutra has no extended metaphor; its organizing motif is enumeration itself — virtue counted in fixed numbered sets (5 senses, 9-fold brahmacharya, 4 kashayas, 5 mahavratas, 5-fold achara, 5 samitis, 3 guptis) that sum to 36. The single sustained 'image' is the composite portrait of the guru assembled from these counts: the teacher is not described by personality but defined by a checklist of disciplines, so that 'guru' means precisely 'one in whom these thirty-six are complete.'
keyThemes: Guru-sthapana: invoking and establishing the guru's presence
The thirty-six attributes (chhattis guna) of an ideal teacher
The fivefold conduct (achara), five carefulnesses (samiti) and three restraints (gupti)

glossary.definition: The five senses (touch, taste, smell, sight, hearing); the guru is 'sanvarano' — one who restrains them.
Restraint or stopping of the inflow of karma; here, holding the senses in check.
The ninefold safeguard of celibacy — nine rules protecting chastity in thought, word and deed.
Restraint or guarding; the three guptis are control of mind, speech and body. 'Guttidharo' = bearer of these guards.
The four passions — anger (krodha), pride (mana), deceit (maya), greed (lobha); the guru is 'mukko', free of them.
The five great vows of an ascetic: non-violence, truth, non-stealing, celibacy, non-possession.
Right conduct; the fivefold achara — in knowledge (jnana), faith (darshana), conduct (charitra), austerity (tapa) and energy (virya).
The five carefulnesses in daily acts: walking, speaking, seeking alms, handling objects, and disposal.
The thirty-six virtues that together define a true guru (18 + 18 across the two verses).
A symbolic representation of the guru (often a small ritual stand) established so the rite is performed in the guru's presence.
The act of invoking and 'installing' the guru at the start of the observance — the purpose of this sutra.
Veneration of the guru; the third of the six Avashyakas, under which this sutra falls.

howToRead: A new learner can read the sutra simply as a two-step tally — eighteen virtues in the first verse, eighteen in the second, thirty-six in all — that paints the picture of a worthy teacher one is about to honour. An advanced practitioner will recognize each numbered set (senses, brahmacharya, kashaya, mahavrata, achara, samiti, gupti) as a doorway into established categories of Jain conduct and will dwell on the guru-sthapana function of reciting it.

sources.title: Pratikraman Sutra with meaning (Panchindiya / guru-sthapana sutra)
sources.consultedFor: Standard decomposition of the 36 guru-attributes (5+9+4 and 5+5+5+3) and the guru-sthapana use of the sutra

notes: The 36-attribute breakdown given here is read directly from the two verses themselves and matches the standard Shvetambara enumeration. No individual author is attested in the source book, so authorship is left open rather than invented. Per the book's orthography conventions, the verbatim Gujarati readings (e.g. અતિયાર-style spellings) are authoritative; the IAST glossary forms above are normalizations for the reader and are not meant to override the book's text. The exact ritual hardware of the sthapanacharya can vary by local practice; the function — establishing the guru's presence — is the constant.
